 Chapter I
Chapter One
I

A Boy on a Train

A Boy on a Train1.1 · The Question at Sixteen

1.1The Question at Sixteen

Ride a bicycle alongside another cyclist at exactly her speed and something ordinary happens: she stops moving. Relative to you she hangs frozen in space, wheels spinning, her handlebar close enough to touch. Every child who has ever matched pace with a friend knows this, and nothing about it is mysterious. Motion is relative; match the motion, and it vanishes. In 1895 a sixteen-year-old at the cantonal school in Aarau asked what should have been an equally innocent question. Light, he had learned, is a wave crossing space at 300,000 kilometers every second. Fast, certainly, but fast is a number, and numbers can be matched. So: sprint after a beam of light and ride beside it, pedal for pedal. What do you see at your shoulder?

The answer the cyclist forces on us is that you should see the wave stopped, its crests laid out in space like a snapshot of the sea, oscillating in place but going nowhere. And here the boy, Albert Einstein, felt the ground give way. What he had at sixteen was an instinct, and the instinct said: no one has ever seen such a thing, and no theory of light has a place for it. A wave that does not travel did not seem to be light at all. Recalling the moment fifty years later, he said there appeared to be no such thing, “neither on the basis of experience nor according to Maxwell’s equations.”

The trap is that chasing something is not supposed to change what it is. Running after the cyclist does not turn her bicycle into a unicycle; sail at wave speed and you really do see the swell frozen beside your hull, and no law of fluid mechanics objects. Chasing is mechanically innocent—the one thing three centuries of physics since Galileo guaranteed you could always do: change your own uniform motion and watch the same laws carry on undisturbed. So the sixteen-year-old held two certainties that could not both survive. Mechanics said: you can chase anything, and the chase changes nothing fundamental. The physics of light said: you can never see a light wave at rest. One carried the authority of Newton, the other of Maxwell, and he could find no error in either.

This chapter is the story of that collision, taken slowly. We assemble its two halves in turn—first the mechanical side, what “motion is relative” actually asserts and how velocities combine when everyone shares one universal clock; then the electromagnetic side, why Maxwell’s theory names a speed with no reference body and why Faraday’s law rules out the frozen wave. We watch every escape route close, each shut by an experiment. Then we do what Einstein did: accept both certainties as postulates and follow what they jointly demand. What they demand, it turns out, is the surrender of universal time itself—the assumption, buried in the denominator of every velocity ever measured, that your clock and mine tick together.

Ask
Euler
Stuck? Scan this pageAsk Euler, your Fermi AI tutor, anything about this page — why a step works, the physical picture, or a worked example. It answers in your pocket.
A Boy on a Train1.2 · Which Train Is Moving?

1.2Which Train Is Moving?

You sit in a train at the station, and the window is filled edge to edge by another train on the next track. There is a soft glide of motion; rivets begin to slip past. For a moment you genuinely cannot tell: is it us or them? The coffee sits flat, the keys hang plumb, nothing inside the carriage tips or presses. Only when the platform swings into view do you learn the answer—and notice the answer came from outside. This is a theorem about what the window can measure. Let u be your velocity and v the neighbor’s, both unknown. The window delivers one number, their velocity relative to you: say v − u = +35 m/s. One equation, two unknowns. Maybe you are parked and they pull away; maybe they are parked and you roll back; maybe both are under way at airliner speeds. The window cannot tell these worlds apart, because it only ever measures the difference v − u.

The platform seems to settle it, but only by importing a velocity from outside, and the regress never ends: the platform is bolted to a planet whose surface at the latitude of Paris moves east at about 310 m/s; the planet orbits the Sun at 30 km/s; the Sun circles the galactic center at roughly 230 km/s. “At rest” is not a property a body has. It is a relationship a body has with some other body, and you can always ask what that other body is doing. The child at the window has discovered that mechanics comes with a built-in blind spot: uniform velocity, measured from inside, is invisible. Galileo was the first to state the blind spot as a law of nature, in the Dialogue of 1632—shut yourself below decks in a great ship with flies and a bowl of fish, let it move uniformly, and nothing betrays the motion.

v − u = +35 m/s1.2.1

Galileo’s central move is that the falling stone shares the ship’s motion, and it carries a lesson that runs through this whole book: two observers in relative motion can give wildly different descriptions of the same fall, and both can be correct. Let a passenger on a train moving at v = 30 m/s drop her keys from 1.25 m. In the train frame the keys fall straight down a 1.25 m vertical line, striking her feet after 0.50 s at 5 m/s. On the platform the same keys are already moving at 30 m/s horizontally, so they trace a 15 m parabola, land 15 m down the platform, and strike at about 30.4 m/s with 37 times the kinetic energy—yet her feet also moved 15 m, so keys and shoes still meet. The frames disagree about path, speed, and energy; they agree about the events. Both solve the same law, F = ma.

Hold on to the split this exposes. Paths, velocities, energies are frame-dependent bookkeeping relative to a chosen reference body, and different frames keep different books. Events are invariant: the keys strike the floor at her feet in every frame, or in none. Physics must be a set of statements about the invariant things, and much of this book is the sorting of quantities into those two piles—a sorting that will hold surprises, since elapsed time and the length of a rod, trusted all your life, are about to move from the invariant pile to the frame-dependent one. The train, the ship, and the keys earn a first principle: P1 (provisional). No mechanical experiment performed entirely within a laboratory in uniform motion can detect that motion. Both words are load-bearing—uniform (brake, and the coffee climbs the cup), and mechanical (whether light respects the same blindness is exactly the open question).

Ask
Euler
Stuck? Scan this pageAsk Euler, your Fermi AI tutor, anything about this page — why a step works, the physical picture, or a worked example. It answers in your pocket.
A Boy on a Train1.3 · Adding Velocities the Honest Way

1.3Adding Velocities the Honest Way

A car passes you on the motorway: you do 100 km/h, it does 120, so it drifts by at 20. A passenger walks toward the front of a train at 1 m/s while the train does 30, and the platform sees 31. What could go wrong? But “obvious” is not a category in physics. Velocity addition is a theorem, and theorems have premises—our job is to expose them one per line, so that when the theorem fails we can point to the premise that gave way. Fix the convention for the whole book: the platform frame S has coordinates (x, t), the train frame S′ has (x′, t′), and S′ moves at +v along the shared x-axis. A point at fixed x′ inside the train sits at platform position x′ + vt, so the Galilean transformation is:

x′ = x − vt, t′ = t. Both lines are written deliberately. The second looks like pedantry—of course the clocks agree, time is time. It is precisely because t′ = t seemed too obvious to write that it went unwritten, and therefore unexamined, for two and a half centuries. Newton at least stated it: “Absolute, true, and mathematical time … flows equably without relation to anything external.” Keep your eye on that line; it is the most consequential equation in this chapter.

x′ = x − vt, t′ = t1.3.1
dt/dt′ = 11.3.2
u′ = u − v, u = u′ + v1.3.3

Now the theorem. A particle traces x(t) for the platform and x′(t′) for the train; each frame defines velocity as its own displacement over its own time, u = dx/dt and u′ = dx′/dt′. By the chain rule u′ = (dx′/dt)(dt/dt′). The first factor is bare calculus on x′ = x − vt: dx′/dt = u − v. The second factor is not calculus at all—from t′ = t we read dt/dt′ = 1, and this single replacement is where the physics lives. It asserts that the interval between two events is the same number of seconds on both clocks, regardless of relative motion. Grant it, and the theorem falls out: u′ = u − v, u = u′ + v. Differentiate once more and the same clock factor gives a′ = a: accelerations match, so F = ma reads the same on the train as on the platform. Velocity addition and Newtonian invariance are not two pillars but one, and its name is absolute time.

Notice something strange about u′ = u − v: it contains no scale. It treats 3 m/s and 3×10⁸ m/s with perfect democracy—subtract v from anything, for any v, forever. Galilean kinematics has no built-in speed against which “fast” could even be defined. Ask it large compared to what? and it has no answer. Maxwell’s equations do.

Ask
Euler
Stuck? Scan this pageAsk Euler, your Fermi AI tutor, anything about this page — why a step works, the physical picture, or a worked example. It answers in your pocket.
A Boy on a Train1.4 · The Light in the Carriage

1.4The Light in the Carriage

The boy switches on the carriage lamp, and with that ordinary act he smuggles aboard the one piece of physics that will not submit to the subtraction rule. By 1865 Maxwell had gathered everything known about electricity and magnetism into four equations, and they contained a surprise: electric and magnetic fields can sustain each other in flight, rippling through empty space as a wave whose speed the equations dictate: c = 1/√(ε₀μ₀). Look at what feeds this formula. The permittivity ε₀ you get from a charged capacitor, the permeability μ₀ from the force between two wires—bench equipment bolted to a table. Put in the measured values and out comes 2.998×10⁸ m/s, matching Fizeau’s measured speed of light to a few percent. Maxwell drew the only sane conclusion: light is a wave of the electromagnetic field.

Now notice what the formula does not contain. There is no v in it, and no slot where a v could go. Ask Newton how fast a cannonball flies and he asks back: launched from what, measured by whom? Ask Maxwell how fast light flies and he hands you one number and falls silent. The vacuum does not come with a speedometer. The equations name a speed without naming an observer—something no equation of mechanics had ever done. Maxwell’s equations only suggest a frame-independent speed; the statement that light travels at c = 299,792,458 m/s in every inertial frame, independent of its source, stands on measurement, and the working hypothesis the nineteenth century was forced to test.

c = 1/√(ε₀μ₀)1.4.1
u′ = u − v = c − v1.4.2

Bring it into the carriage and watch the collision. The train moves at +v, the lamp fires a pulse forward, the platform measures it at u = c. The boy applies direct subtraction: u′ = u − v = c − v. But if motion-blindness extends to electromagnetism, the boy’s own capacitors and coils return the same ε₀ and μ₀, and his own Maxwell equations demand he measure c. Then c − v = c, true only for v = 0. For any moving carriage whatsoever, Galilean addition and the constancy of c contradict each other flatly. These are not two estimates differing in the third decimal; they are two certainties, one a theorem of absolute time and one a fact of the laboratory, and both cannot stand.

The immediate objection is quantitative: the discrepancy is of order v/c, one part in ten million for a train—could it hide below the noise floor? It could not, because nature provides a far faster carriage. The Earth orbits the Sun at v = 2.98×10⁴ m/s, so v/c ≈ 10⁻⁴ and (v/c)² ≈ 10⁻⁸. In a round-trip light experiment the first-order shifts nearly cancel, leaving a second-order survivor of order (v/c)²; over a 10 m path that is a discrepancy near 100 nm, a fifth of a wavelength of visible light—within reach of an 1880s interferometer, and better still, the signature should reverse every six months as Earth’s velocity flips. The experiment was performable, and it was performed.

Ask
Euler
Stuck? Scan this pageAsk Euler, your Fermi AI tutor, anything about this page — why a step works, the physical picture, or a worked example. It answers in your pocket.
A Boy on a Train1.5 · The Frozen Wave, Killed by Faraday

1.5The Frozen Wave, Killed by Faraday

Now the retrospective audit the sixteen-year-old could not yet run. Take the beam to be a monochromatic plane wave running down the x-axis, E(x,t) = ŷ E₀ cos(kx − ωt), with the dispersion relation ω = ck that Maxwell’s equations enforce; holding the phase fixed gives crest speed ω/k = c. Put a runner at +v, with x = x′ + vt′ and t = t′. Substituting into the phase gives k′ = k but ω′ = ω − kv = k(c − v). The wavenumber is untouched—Galilean observers carry identical rulers and agree on simultaneous snapshots—but the frequency drains away linearly with chase speed, the Galilean Doppler shift, and the wave speed the runner assigns is ω′/k′ = c − v: the direct subtraction wearing wave clothing. Push v all the way to c and ω′ hits zero: the runner sees a sinusoidal ripple painted onto space, the same at every instant—crests frozen, fields static, precisely the object the boy pictured.

Now Maxwell’s equations vote, through Faraday’s law: ∇×E = −∂B/∂t. A circulating electric field exists only where magnetic flux is changing; the law is exact and local. Apply it to the frozen wave. The pattern is static, so the right-hand side vanishes identically. But the left-hand side does not: for E′ = ŷ E₀ cos(kx′) the curl is −ẑ kE₀ sin(kx′), nonzero at almost every point. Faraday’s law demands zero and gets −kE₀ sin(kx′). The frozen wave is not an exotic solution, or a rare one, or an unstable one—it is not a solution at all, impossible the way a four-sided triangle is impossible.

ω′ = ω − kv = k(c − v)1.5.1
∇×E = −∂B/∂t1.5.2
ω′ = ±ck1.5.3

One can push the audit to the end. Insert the general ansatz into the two curl equations: Faraday demands kE₀ = ω′B₀, Ampère–Maxwell demands kB₀ = ω′E₀/c², and multiplying gives ω′ = ±ck for any wave with nonzero amplitudes. Maxwell’s equations do not merely permit speed c; they compel it, for every wave, in every frame where the equations hold. Impose the runner’s verdict ω′ = 0 with k ≠ 0 and both amplitudes are forced to vanish. The only frozen light is darkness.

Hold the two halves side by side. The derivation of ω′ = k(c − v) was valid: substitute, expand, read off. Its conclusion is impossible: electromagnetism forbids the object it describes. A valid derivation with an impossible conclusion indicts a premise, and this one used two: x′ = x − vt and t′ = t. The spatial premise delivered k′ = k, and Maxwell has no complaint against it. The temporal premise delivered the drained frequency, and frequency is a ratio—cycles counted against ticks of a clock. When the ratio misbehaves and the numerator is sound, suspicion falls on the denominator. The clock is the first premise indicted. So the boy gets his answer, though not the one he wanted: his errand was not difficult, it was impossible, and impossible in an informative way. There is no frame in which a light wave stands still, because a standing light wave is not an electromagnetic object.

Ask
Euler
Stuck? Scan this pageAsk Euler, your Fermi AI tutor, anything about this page — why a step works, the physical picture, or a worked example. It answers in your pocket.
A Boy on a Train1.6 · Failed Escapes

1.6Failed Escapes

A structural contradiction deserves better than immediate surrender, and physics owed the old framework every conceivable escape. There are two ways to dodge the collision without touching the clocks: either light inherits the speed of its source, or Maxwell’s equations hold in one privileged frame only. Emission theory, developed by Ritz in 1908, supposes light leaves its source at c relative to the source, like a thrown ball. Galilean addition is saved instantly. But astronomy tests it wholesale: for a binary star at distance D with line-of-sight speed ±v, fast light from the approaching phase would overtake slow light emitted orbits earlier, painting ghost images across the orbit, with transit-time difference Δt ≈ (2D/c)(v/c)—days or weeks for a distant binary that orbits in days. In 1913 de Sitter surveyed the well-observed binaries: clean Keplerian ellipses, no ghosts. Brecher’s 1977 pulsar analysis bounds any source-velocity contribution below two parts in 10⁹. Light does not care how its source moves.

The second dodge concedes that light’s speed belongs to a medium and then insists there is one: a luminiferous ether filling all space, so Maxwell’s c is the speed relative to the ether. But if the ether is not dragged, Earth’s orbital motion puts every lab in an ether wind of at least 30 km/s, and light behaves like a swimmer in a river. Down an arm of length L aligned with the motion the round trip costs t∥ = (2L/c) · 1/(1 − v²/c²); across it, t⊥ = (2L/c) · 1/√(1 − v²/c²). The two trips are unequal, in the ratio 1/√(1 − v²/c²)—a factor destined to haunt this whole book, here making its first appearance as the ether wind’s fingerprint. The time difference is second order, Δt ≈ (L/c)(v²/c²), which is why only interferometry could hope to see it.

Δt ≈ (2D/c)(v/c)1.6.1
t∥ = (2L/c) · 1/(1 − v²/c²)1.6.2
t⊥ = (2L/c) · 1/√(1 − v²/c²)1.6.3

In 1887 Michelson and Morley built exactly the instrument, splitting sodium light along two perpendicular arms folded to L = 11 m, floating on mercury so it could rotate. A 90° rotation should shift the fringes by about 0.4 fringe; their resolution was 0.01 fringe—the prediction stood forty times above the noise. They rotated the slab through July 1887 and repeated months later. The fringes never moved. The ether wind blows at nothing. What followed was a forty-five-year siege of patches: FitzGerald and Lorentz proposed an ad hoc contraction of the parallel arm to hide the wind; Kennedy and Thorndike (1932) closed that door with unequal arms where contraction alone cannot keep the null through the year. Every patch that saved the ether from one experiment was contradicted by another.

Tally the escapes. Source-dependent light: dead, to parts per billion. An ether wind: absent at every season. A dragged ether: forbidden by aberration and Fizeau’s partial drag. A conspiracy of lengths: caught by Kennedy–Thorndike. The only door left is the one marked revise the postulates—accept both certainties, and let universal time pay the bill. Every classical rescue of absolute time made a quantitative prediction, and every prediction failed. Every inertial observer, however moving, measures the same c.

Euler

A Boy on a Train1.7 · Rods, Clocks, and the First Picture

1.7Rods, Clocks, and the First Picture

We are preparing to convict universal time, and a conviction requires the charge stated precisely. “Moving clocks run slow” is a slogan until we say what a clock reading and a length measurement are. A frame is furniture, not a viewpoint. Fill space with a rigid scaffolding of identical meter sticks laid end to end, and at every node bolt a clock of identical manufacture. The lattice earns the adjective inertial by a test: free particles released at rest stay at rest, and moving ones coast in straight lines. An event—a firecracker popping, a photon striking a detector—is assigned coordinates locally: its position is the address of the nearest node, its time the reading of the clock bolted at that node. Nothing travels; no one watches from afar. The records can be couriered a week later; the timestamps were made on the spot.

One problem remains, and it is the deep one: synchronizing the clocks. Identical clocks tick at the same rate, but one started at the wrong moment reads wrong forever, and every naive fix fails—you cannot glance across the lattice (the glance arrives late), and you cannot correct for the delay by dividing distance by the one-way speed of light (the one-way speed is exactly what unsynchronized clocks cannot measure). The circle is cut by a definition, the midpoint procedure: find the midpoint of AB with rods, fire a flash there, and let each clock set itself to zero on arrival. Equivalently, A emits at reading t₁, the pulse reflects off B and returns at t₃, and B is synchronized if its reading at reflection was t_B = (t₁ + t₃)/2.

t_B = (t₁ + t₃)/21.7.1

Both procedures rest on one stipulation: light’s speed is the same in both directions. The vacuum offers no arrow to prefer, so this is the uniquely symmetric choice—but be honest about its status. A single clock and a mirror deliver the round-trip speed 2L/(t₃ − t₁), a convention-free number, and the round trip is what every experiment actually reported. The one-way speed contains t_B, a reading on a second clock our procedure defined so as to make it equal the round-trip value. A one-way speed is a fact about light plus a clock convention. Nothing scandalous—but the distinction will keep us honest later, when observers in different frames start disagreeing about which distant events are simultaneous. Their disagreement traces back to this line: each frame synchronizes its own lattice by its own midpoint flashes.

Now the first picture. The ledger is a list of events, and pairs beg to be plotted: position horizontally, time vertically—and measured as ct rather than t, so both axes carry meters and a light ray becomes a 45° line. This is a spacetime diagram. An event is a point; an object persisting through time is a curve, its worldline; a parked clock is a vertical line; an object at speed u has slope c/u, nearly vertical for everyday speeds and tilting toward 45° as u → c. A horizontal slice is the lattice’s “now.” The equipment is complete—rods compared against a standard, clocks synchronized by flashes, events stamped locally, worldlines plotted—and note what we have not assumed: that two lattices sliding past one another agree on the time between two events or the length of a rod. Galileo assumed it; nothing in the apparatus requires it.

Ask
Euler
Stuck? Scan this pageAsk Euler, your Fermi AI tutor, anything about this page — why a step works, the physical picture, or a worked example. It answers in your pocket.
A Boy on a Train1.8 · The Two Postulates

1.8The Two Postulates

Everything so far has been demolition. Galilean addition collided with Maxwell; the frozen wave died at Faraday’s hands; emission theory and the ether each contradicted an observation. What remains standing are two statements, and the natural move is to write them down as postulates and build on nothing else. Postulate 1 (Relativity): the laws of physics take the same form in every inertial frame. Postulate 2 (Light): light in vacuum travels at speed c in every inertial frame, regardless of the motion of its source or of the observer.

Read them slowly. P1 you met in a weaker form covering mechanical experiments only; the postulate deletes the word mechanical. All laws—mechanics, electromagnetism, everything physicists would discover in the following century—must read the same in every inertial frame. A bold extension, and a forced one: if electromagnetism obeyed different equations in different frames, a lamp and a coil would constitute a motion detector, and the failed escapes cataloged precisely such detectors failing at sensitivities forty times beyond what the ether demanded.

P2 looks like the shocking one, but look at what it claims. Independence of the source’s motion is what de Sitter’s binaries showed; independence of the observer’s motion is what Michelson and Morley confirmed season after season; and c = 1/√(ε₀μ₀) is Maxwell’s gift, a speed manufactured from vacuum constants with no slot for an observer’s v. P2 is not a conjecture bolted onto the evidence; it is the minimal transcription of the evidence. A final word about temperament: the postulates are routinely introduced as a shocking break with common sense, but this gets it backwards. Each is the plainest summary of what experiments said. What common sense actually demands is a third, unspoken postulate—that dt = dt′, that all lattices share one clock—and that is the one the evidence has now indicted. Everything strange downstream is not new physics being injected; it is old, unexamined bookkeeping being corrected.

Ask
Euler
Stuck? Scan this pageAsk Euler, your Fermi AI tutor, anything about this page — why a step works, the physical picture, or a worked example. It answers in your pocket.
A Boy on a Train1.9 · The Price and the Prize

1.9The Price and the Prize

The bill arrives as a single calculation: a pulse of light bouncing once along a moving carriage, timed by two lattices. Worked example—the light in the carriage, timed twice. A carriage of length L = 12 m has a lamp at the rear wall and a mirror at the front; the train moves at v = 0.6c. On board the walls do not move, so by P2 the pulse crosses L each way at c: Δt′ = 2L/c = 80 ns. On the platform both walls move, and—P2 with full force—the pulse still travels at c, not c ± v. Outbound the mirror retreats, closing the 12 m gap at c − v = 0.4c: 100 ns. Returning, the rear wall advances, closing at c + v = 1.6c: 25 ns. Total Δt = (2L/c) · 1/(1 − v²/c²) = 80 ns / 0.64 = 125 ns, which checks against the legs: 100 + 25.

As v/c → 0 both frames report 80 ns, the Newtonian answer, with corrections of order v²/c²—at real train speed one part in 10¹⁴, an error of about 8×10⁻²² s on an 80 ns measurement. No clock of Newton’s era, or Maxwell’s, could come within twelve orders of magnitude of resolving it. The two frames assign 80 ns and 125 ns to the interval between the same two events. Under the postulates, “the platform measures length L” and “both lattices measure the same elapsed time” cannot both stand, for together they yield 125 = 80. At least one of universal time and universal length is false—and the next chapter shows the factor 1/(1 − v²/c²) splitting into two equal pieces of 1/√(1 − v²/c²) each: one paid by moving clocks running slow, one by moving lengths contracting.

Δt′ = 2L/c = 80 ns1.9.1
Δt = (2L/c) · 1/(1 − v²/c²) = 125 ns1.9.2

That is the price: “how long did it take?” and “how far apart are they?” no longer have observer-independent answers. Elapsed time and length join the keys’ trajectory—vertical line for one observer, parabola for another—as quantities that are real but frame-relative. But here is the prize, and it is worth more than what was paid. The disagreements between frames are not chaos; they are as tightly choreographed as the two shadows of one rod, which trade off so as to preserve the one length the rod actually has. The 80 and the 125 nanoseconds will turn out to be such shadows: different projections, taken by differently moving observers, of a single frame-independent object stretched between the two events. Spacetime has a geometry of its own, with its own invariant notion of separation. The postulates did not take away universal time and give back nothing. They traded a false absolute for a true one.
