 Chapter II
Chapter Two
II

Time Dilation and Length Contraction

Time Dilation and Length Contraction2.1 · The Simplest Clock in the Universe

2.1The Simplest Clock in the Universe

Every clock is a repeating process plus a counter: a pendulum swings and an escapement counts, a cesium atom oscillates and nine billion oscillations make a second. The physics of the repeating process sets the rate—and there lies a trap. If we want to ask what motion does to time itself, we had better not use a clock whose ticking depends on springs or crystal lattices, because then every strange result invites the lawyerly escape: “motion didn’t affect time; it merely strained the spring.” We need a clock so simple there is nothing inside it to strain.

Here it is. Take two mirrors facing each other, held a fixed distance L apart by a rigid rod. Launch a single flash from the bottom mirror; it travels up, reflects, travels down, and returns. That round trip is one tick: a detector clicks, a counter increments, and the flash is sent up again. Two mirrors, one flash, one counter—no moving parts except light itself, and light is not negotiable. Sit in the frame where the clock is at rest and time one tick. The flash covers L up and L down, always at speed c, so one tick lasts Δt₀ = 2L/c. With L = 1.5 m that is a ten-nanosecond tick.

Δt₀ = 2L/c2.1.1

The reading Δt₀ has a name we will use for the rest of the book. The proper time between two events is the time recorded by a single clock present at both of them—here, the flash’s departure and its return, which happen at the same place in the clock’s frame. Proper time is a purely local fact: two glances at one dial, and no frame can dispute a dial reading.

Ask
Euler
Stuck? Scan this pageAsk Euler, your Fermi AI tutor, anything about this page — why a step works, the physical picture, or a worked example. It answers in your pocket.
Time Dilation and Length Contraction2.2 · The Same Clock, Timed from the Platform

2.2The Same Clock, Timed from the Platform

Now let the clock ride a train past a platform at speed v, its mirrors mounted transverse to the motion—the rod stands vertical while the train slides horizontally. In the train frame nothing has changed: one tick still takes 2L/c. But to the platform the flash does not go straight up and down. While it climbs, the whole apparatus slides forward, so the top mirror is no longer overhead; the flash must travel a diagonal to catch it, then a second diagonal down. The path is a zigzag, and its two legs are plainly longer than the straight 2L.

Put numbers on the triangle. Let Δt be the platform’s tick. In that time the flash travels total path cΔt (second postulate: light moves at c for the platform too), so each leg is cΔt/2. Meanwhile the clock advances vΔt, so each leg’s horizontal run is vΔt/2. The vertical rise is still L, transverse to the motion and unchanged. Pythagoras on one leg: (cΔt/2)² = L² + (vΔt/2)². Solve: (1/4)(c² − v²)Δt² = L², so Δt = 2L/√(c² − v²) = γΔt₀, with γ = 1/√(1 − v²/c²).

(cΔt/2)² = L² + (vΔt/2)²2.2.1
Δt = γΔt₀, γ = 1/√(1 − v²/c²)2.2.2
Scan →
derivation
▶ Watch it derivedScan to play the Time dilation, from the light clock as a short video — every line above built on screen, step by step, in the book’s own hand.

The platform’s tick is longer than the train’s by the factor γ, which is never less than 1. A moving clock is measured to run slow. This is time dilation, and notice where it came from: not from any assumption about clocks, only from the geometry of a longer light path traversed at the same c. The one and only physical input was the second postulate.

Worked example: the tick of a fast clock. A light clock ticks every Δt₀ = 10.0 ns in its rest frame and flies past a lab at v = 0.60c. Then γ = 1/√(1 − 0.36) = 1/0.80 = 1.25, so Δt = 1.25 × 10.0 = 12.5 ns. Since γ > 1, the lab’s tick is longer than the clock’s own—the moving clock runs slow, as it must. There is nothing wrong with the clock: run it in the lab and it ticks at 10.0 ns like any other. The extra 2.5 ns is what the lab’s synchronized clocks record for a process happening somewhere that is moving.

Ask
Euler
Stuck? Scan this pageAsk Euler, your Fermi AI tutor, anything about this page — why a step works, the physical picture, or a worked example. It answers in your pocket.
Time Dilation and Length Contraction2.3 · Anatomy of Gamma

2.3Anatomy of Gamma

Everything quantitative in relativity is bolted to the one function γ(β) = 1/√(1 − β²), so it is worth knowing its face. At everyday speeds γ is indistinguishable from 1. Expanding for small β gives γ ≈ 1 + ½β²: the departure from 1 is second order in β, and that single fact governs the whole low-speed world. A jetliner at 250 m/s has β ≈ 8×10⁻⁷, so γ − 1 ≈ 3×10⁻¹³—a clock aboard loses about 30 nanoseconds per year. This is why twenty-three centuries of physics never noticed: the correction is quadratic in a ratio that is minuscule for anything you can throw, drive, or fly. Relativity does not overturn Newton at low speed; it contains him as the leading term.

Near c, γ climbs without bound. At β = 0.9 it is 2.3; at 0.99, 7.1; at 0.999, 22; and as β → 1, γ → ∞. The divergence is not a glitch—it is the reason c is a speed limit. Every increment of speed past 0.99c costs a disproportionate exertion. And there is no threshold: γ exceeds 1 at every nonzero speed; it is simply too close to 1 to matter until β is a sizable fraction of one. GPS satellites at a mere β ≈ 1.3×10⁻⁵ still need the correction, because their clocks are read to nanoseconds. “Relativistic” is not a regime of nature; it is a regime of precision.

γ(β) = 1/√(1 − β²)2.3.1
γ ≈ 1 + ½β² (small β)2.3.2
Ask
Euler
Stuck? Scan this pageAsk Euler, your Fermi AI tutor, anything about this page — why a step works, the physical picture, or a worked example. It answers in your pocket.
Time Dilation and Length Contraction2.4 · Not a Trick of the Clock

2.4Not a Trick of the Clock

A skeptic will object that we have proven something about light clocks, not about time. Perhaps only bouncing photons slow down, while pendulums, heartbeats, and atoms carry on regardless. The first postulate demolishes this in one move. Suppose a light clock and a wristwatch sit side by side on the train, ticking in perfect step. View them from the platform. If the light clock dilated by γ but the wristwatch did not, the two would drift out of step, and the passenger, watching her own two clocks disagree, could read off her speed through space from the drift rate. That is an experiment done entirely inside the sealed train, and it returns an absolute velocity—exactly what the principle of relativity forbids. The escape is forced: every process on the train, mechanical, chemical, biological, nuclear, must dilate by the identical γ.

Time dilation is not a fact about light clocks. It is a fact about the rate of every clock, because “the rate of time” in a frame is just what all its clocks agree on. The light clock was scaffolding; from here on, “a clock” means anything periodic—including a particle counting down to its own decay.

One discipline this section quietly assumes deserves to be explicit: measuring is not seeing. What a single eye or camera records is the light that happens to reach it now, carrying its own travel delays and Doppler shifts. A frame’s measurement of a duration is what its grid of synchronized clocks reports after the fact, with all light-travel time already subtracted. Time dilation is a statement about that grid, not about any snapshot. Every “sees” in this chapter is shorthand for “measures with local synchronized clocks.”

Ask
Euler
Stuck? Scan this pageAsk Euler, your Fermi AI tutor, anything about this page — why a step works, the physical picture, or a worked example. It answers in your pocket.
Time Dilation and Length Contraction2.5 · The Muon at Sea Level

2.5The Muon at Sea Level

Nature builds the clock we need and drops it on us for free. Cosmic-ray protons strike nuclei about 15 km up; the debris includes muons—particles like electrons but 207 times heavier and unstable, living on average τ₀ = 2.2 μs at rest before decaying. A stopped muon is a clock read in its own rest frame, so τ₀ is a proper time. Decay is statistical: a population dwindles as N(t) = N₀ e^(−t/τ₀), where t is time on the muons’ own clocks. That exponential makes the muon a merciless referee—it magnifies any error in the elapsed time into a huge error in the survivor count.

Worked example: two predictions, one detector. Muons are created at altitude h = 15 km moving straight down at v = 0.995c. The trip time in the ground frame is t = h/v = 50.3 μs. Newtonian prediction (no dilation): the muon ages 50.3/2.2 = 22.8 lifetimes, surviving fraction e^(−22.8) ≈ 1.2×10⁻¹⁰—one in ten billion, so the detectors should sit silent. Relativistic prediction: with γ = 1/√(1 − 0.995²) ≈ 10.0, the muon’s own elapsed time is only t/γ = 5.0 μs = 2.3 lifetimes, surviving fraction e^(−2.3) ≈ 0.10. The two predictions differ by a factor of eight hundred million—this is not a subtle test.

τ₀ = 2.2 μs2.5.1
N(t) = N₀ e^(−t/τ₀)2.5.2
Scan →
derivation
▶ Watch it derivedScan to play the The muon at sea level as a short video — every line above built on screen, step by step, in the book’s own hand.

Detectors at sea level register muons in floods—about one through your palm every second—in quantitative agreement with the relativistic figure. The muon’s internal clock really did tick only 5 μs while 50 μs passed on the ground. In 1963 Frisch and Smith made it classroom-clean: they counted muons in a chosen speed band atop Mount Washington (1910 m) and again at sea level in Cambridge, and the survival ratio matched e^(−Δt/γτ₀) to within error. One mountain, two detectors, and a stopwatch nature built—the cleanest tabletop verdict for time dilation ever staged. A real, non-optical clock dilates by exactly the γ the light clock predicted.

Ask
Euler
Stuck? Scan this pageAsk Euler, your Fermi AI tutor, anything about this page — why a step works, the physical picture, or a worked example. It answers in your pocket.
Time Dilation and Length Contraction2.6 · Clocks Out of Step: The Synchronization Offset

2.6Clocks Out of Step: The Synchronization Offset

Every measurement in this chapter has hidden an accomplice. To time one tick of the moving light clock, the platform cannot use a single stopwatch: the tick starts at one place and ends at another, so the measurement needs two platform clocks with their readings subtracted—which silently assumes those two clocks agree about what time it is. The question nobody has asked: if the train synchronizes its clocks flawlessly by its own lights, does the platform agree the job was done right?

Put a lamp at the midpoint of a car of rest length L, with a clock at each wall. In the train frame the walls are equidistant from the lamp, light travels at c both ways, so the flashes arrive together and the wall clocks are synchronized—perfect, by the train’s lights. Now watch from the platform, where the car moves forward at v. The light still spreads at c in this frame, but the walls no longer sit still while it travels: the rear wall advances to meet its flash and is lit first; the front wall flees its own and is lit later. The two wall clocks, which the train set going “together,” are started at different platform instants.

Δt_offset = vL/c² (rear clock ahead)2.6.1
Scan →
derivation
▶ Watch it derivedScan to play the Leading clocks lag: the synchronization offset as a short video — every line above built on screen, step by step, in the book’s own hand.

We need no new machinery to price the offset. The two clock-starting events are simultaneous in the train frame and lie a proper distance L apart along the track—exactly the pair the relativity of simultaneity weighs, whose verdict is that a frame in which they move finds them out of step by vL/c², the rear event first. Each clock then runs on at the same steady rate, so the head start never closes: Δt_offset = vL/c², with the rear clock reading ahead of the front, L the rest-frame separation, in any frame where they move. This leans only on the relativity of simultaneity—not on time dilation, and not on length contraction, which we have not yet derived.

Ask
Euler
Stuck? Scan this pageAsk Euler, your Fermi AI tutor, anything about this page — why a step works, the physical picture, or a worked example. It answers in your pocket.
Time Dilation and Length Contraction2.7 · Whose Clock Is Slow? The Reciprocity Audit

2.7Whose Clock Is Slow? The Reciprocity Audit

The derivation of time dilation never used any property of the platform—only a light clock at rest in one frame and an observer watching from another. So the passenger can run it in reverse: to her the platform rushes past at v, a light clock on the station wall traces the same zigzag, and the same triangle delivers the same verdict. The platform says train clocks run slow by γ; the train says platform clocks run slow by γ. This is the sentence nobody believes on first hearing—two clocks each slower than the other sounds like two people each taller than the other. The cure is to open the books.

Worked example: the reciprocity audit at v = 0.8c, γ = 5/3. Platform clocks A and B stand L = 4.0 light-seconds apart, Einstein-synchronized. A single train clock passes A (both zeroed), then passes B. Platform ledger: the trip covers 4.0 ls at 0.8c, so Δt_plat = 5.0 s and B reads 5.0 s; the train clock runs slow, reading 5.0 × 0.6 = 3.0 s. Train ledger: the platform streams backward, so (rate) each platform clock ticks slow at 0.6 s per train-second; (offset) the platform clocks are out of step by vL/c² = 3.2 s, rear clock ahead, and B is the rear clock, so when A reads 0, B already reads 3.2 s; (duration) the trip occupies 3.0 s of train time. During those 3.0 s, B advances 3.0 × 0.6 = 1.8 s, but it started at 3.2 s, so its face at event 2 is 1.8 + 3.2 = 5.0 s.

1.8 s (slow ticking) + 3.2 s (head start) = 5.0 s2.7.1

Both ledgers predict the identical dial readings—train 3.0, clock B 5.0—while the train observer insists, correctly by her own measurements, that every platform clock ran slow the whole time. B reads more not because it ticked fast but because, in her frame, it was set 3.2 s ahead before the race began. Notice precisely what the two frames disagree about. Not the events: at both meetings the touching dials agree. The disagreement is entirely about simultaneity at a distance—what clock A reads “at the same moment as” event 2. The platform says 5.0 s; the train says 1.8 s. But those are two different events on A’s history, and the frames are simply pairing event 2 with different partners. Reciprocal dilation is not one measurement with two answers; it is two different measurements, each impeccable in its own frame.

Ask
Euler
Stuck? Scan this pageAsk Euler, your Fermi AI tutor, anything about this page — why a step works, the physical picture, or a worked example. It answers in your pocket.
Time Dilation and Length Contraction2.8 · Rotating the Clock: Length Contraction

2.8Rotating the Clock: Length Contraction

Every light clock so far has ticked sideways. Rotate the apparatus ninety degrees, so the light runs parallel to the motion—chasing the front mirror, then doubling back to the rear—and something new appears. Before any algebra, the first postulate makes a demand. Inside the train, rotating a clock is an experiment in a sealed lab; if the tick rate depended on the clock’s orientation, the passenger could detect her absolute motion by turning a light clock and watching it drift against a second—the forbidden internal speedometer. So in the train frame the longitudinal and transverse clocks tick in perfect unison, both at 2L₀/c. Coincident ticks are local events, so the platform too must find the two clocks ticking at the same rate. That single sentence is a loaded weapon.

Work in the platform frame and call L the mirror separation the platform measures—we do not yet know whether it equals L₀. The flash leaves the rear mirror and chases the front, which flees at v; the gap closes at c − v, so the chase takes L/(c − v). On the return the rear mirror rushes toward the flash, closing at c + v: L/(c + v). Adding, Δt∥ = L/(c−v) + L/(c+v) = (2L/c) · 1/(1 − v²/c²) = (2L/c)γ². There is the surprise: a factor of γ², not γ. But the transverse clock ticks at γ · 2L₀/c, and locality forced the two rates equal. Set them equal: (2L/c)γ² = γ(2L₀/c), giving L = L₀/γ = L₀√(1 − v²/c²).

Δt∥ = (2L/c)γ²2.8.1
L = L₀/γ = L₀√(1 − v²/c²)2.8.2
Scan →
derivation
▶ Watch it derivedScan to play the Length contraction, by rotating the clock as a short video — every line above built on screen, step by step, in the book’s own hand.

The only escape hatch is space itself. To keep the rotated clock honest, the platform must measure the mirror separation shortened by γ. A moving object is measured to be contracted along its direction of motion. The length L₀ measured in the object’s rest frame is its proper length; every frame in which it moves measures less. Length contraction is reciprocal exactly as dilation is: the passenger measures the platform’s meter sticks shortened by γ, and there is no contradiction, because measuring a moving stick means marking its two ends simultaneously, and the frames disagree about simultaneity by precisely the vL/c² offset. Contraction, dilation, and the synchronization offset are three faces of one coin; drop any one and the other two contradict.

Ask
Euler
Stuck? Scan this pageAsk Euler, your Fermi AI tutor, anything about this page — why a step works, the physical picture, or a worked example. It answers in your pocket.
Time Dilation and Length Contraction2.9 · The Quantity Neither Effect Can Touch

2.9The Quantity Neither Effect Can Touch

Physics has a standing move for moments like this: when every measurement depends on your point of view, hunt for the combination that does not. Rotate graph paper and a thumbtack’s x and y both change—but x² + y², its squared distance from the pivot, is the same for every orientation. The distance is real; the coordinates are bookkeeping. We have watched Δt and Δx behave exactly like coordinates: time stretches by γ, length shrinks by γ, and no frame’s numbers are privileged. Is there a “distance” underneath—one frame-proof number for a pair of events? (First we dispose of a loose end: transverse lengths do not change. Two hoops of equal proper diameter on a collision course—if transverse length contracted, each frame would measure the other narrower and one hoop would pass inside the other, but which passes inside is a frame-free fact a photograph settles. Reciprocity plus a frame-free outcome is a contradiction unless the transverse dimension does not change at all.)

Now the invariant. Take one tick of the light clock: in the clock’s rest frame the separation is pure time, Δt₀ = 2L/c with Δx₀ = 0. In a frame where the clock slides at v, both grow: Δt = γΔt₀ and Δx = vΔt = γvΔt₀. The sum (cΔt)² + (Δx)² only inflates faster with v, but the two inflations are fed by the same γ, so a difference can hold still. Try it: (cΔt)² − (Δx)² = γ²Δt₀²(c² − v²) = c²Δt₀². The v-dependence cancels identically—not to leading order, but symbol for symbol. Every inertial frame gets the same value (cΔt₀)² for the combination, and that value is the proper time in disguise: a dial reading everyone can verify by walking up to the clock.

(cΔt)² − (Δx)² = c²Δt₀²2.9.1
Δs² = (cΔt)² − (Δx)²2.9.2

A second case costs nothing. Let the two events be the emission and arrival of a single light flash. Then in every frame Δx = cΔt—that is the second postulate verbatim—so (cΔt)² − (Δx)² = 0 everywhere. Give the quantity a name: the spacetime interval Δs² = (cΔt)² − (Δx)². We have proven it invariant for two kinds of pair—clock ticks and light rays—and the invariant-interval chapter proves it for every pair, once the full Lorentz transformation is in hand.

Turn the logic around and it turns profound. A conserved combination under a family of transformations is the fingerprint of a geometry: from x² + y² alone you could reconstruct circles, angles, perpendicularity. Sweep v from 0 toward c and plot (Δx, cΔt) for one clock tick, and every frame’s point lands on the curve (cΔt)² − (Δx)² = (cΔt₀)²—a hyperbola, racing up its branch toward the asymptote the light line itself traces. One tick, viewed from all frames, paints the same shape a rotating rod paints on graph paper—with a single sign flipped. That minus sign opens the closed circle of space into the open hyperbola of spacetime. And once you see the geometry, dilation and contraction stop being two surprises: a tick of a moving clock and the span of a moving rod are projections of four-dimensional objects onto one observer’s axes, and change frames and the projections change while the object, measured by the interval, is untouched. As Minkowski said in Cologne in 1908, “space by itself, and time by itself, are doomed to fade away into mere shadows.”

Ask
Euler
Stuck? Scan this pageAsk Euler, your Fermi AI tutor, anything about this page — why a step works, the physical picture, or a worked example. It answers in your pocket.
Time Dilation and Length Contraction2.10 · Time Dilation in Your Pocket

2.10Time Dilation in Your Pocket

None of this is exotic. The Global Positioning System is a grid of atomic clocks in orbit, and it works only because engineers pay the relativistic bill to the nanosecond. A GPS satellite orbits at about 3.9 km/s, so β ≈ 1.3×10⁻⁵ and γ − 1 ≈ ½β² ≈ 8×10⁻¹¹. That looks negligible until you convert it to a day.

Worked example: why GPS must know relativity. The fractional rate loss ½β² ≈ 8.4×10⁻¹¹, times 86,400 s per day, is about 7.2 μs of clock slowdown daily. A position fix multiplies clock error by c: 7.2×10⁻⁶ × 3.0×10⁸ ≈ 2.2 km. The sign is right—the moving satellite clock runs slow by the velocity effect—and kilometers per day is exactly the drift GPS engineers report if relativity is switched off. Left uncorrected, your navigator would place you a couple of kilometers off within hours. (The full GPS correction also adds a general-relativistic gravitational term of opposite sign and larger size, but the velocity piece here is pure special relativity, and it is not optional.)

γ − 1 ≈ ½β² ≈ 8×10⁻¹¹2.10.1

The muon and the satellite make the same point from opposite ends of the speed range: time dilation is not a philosopher’s puzzle but a working part of twentieth- and twenty-first-century engineering. A theory that miscounted it would misplace you on the map.

Ask
Euler
Stuck? Scan this pageAsk Euler, your Fermi AI tutor, anything about this page — why a step works, the physical picture, or a worked example. It answers in your pocket.
 Chapter III
Chapter Three
III

Simultaneity

Simultaneity3.1 · The Question Hidden in “At the Same Time”

3.1The Question Hidden in “At the Same Time”

“The two events happened at the same time.” Few sentences sound more harmless, yet try to say what you would actually do to check it and the sentence starts to squirm. If the two events occur at the same place—two firecrackers at your feet—there is no problem: coincidence at a point is direct perception, and every observer agrees. But pull the events apart, one in Lisbon, one in Kyoto, and “at the same time” becomes a claim comparing readings on two separated clocks. It silently presumes those clocks agree about “now,” which is precisely the thing we are trying to establish.

A first attempt: stand somewhere, watch both events, and correct each arrival for the light’s travel time, t_event = t_seen − d/c→, where c→ is the one-way speed of the light coming from it. Reasonable, until you ask how c→ was measured. A one-way speed requires a clock at the start and a clock at the finish, and those two clocks must already be synchronized—which is the very task the correction was invented to perform. The definition chases its own tail: simultaneity needs the one-way speed of light, and the one-way speed of light needs simultaneity. This loop is not a technicality; it is the crack through which this entire chapter will pour.

t_event = t_seen − d/c→3.1.1

For two hundred years the crack was invisible, because Newton had paved over it: “Absolute, true, and mathematical time … flows equably without relation to anything external.” At terrestrial speeds the assumption is harmless. The first cracks appeared not in physics departments but in engineers’ ledgers—the problem of longitude, which is really a problem of time. When the transatlantic cable of 1866 linked observatories, signals arrived tenths of a second late, and the operators’ remedy was to exchange signals both ways and split the difference—assuming, without ceremony, that the signal traveled equally fast both ways. The world’s astronomers had legislated a definition of distant simultaneity, and nobody could prove the two one-way times were equal.

Henri Poincaré, serving on the Bureau des Longitudes, turned the expedient into a verdict in 1898: “We have not a direct intuition of simultaneity.” Distant simultaneity is not discovered; it is defined, and the definition should be chosen so that natural laws come out simplest. He circled the threshold—conventional simultaneity, synchronization by light signals, even the phrase “principle of relativity”—but never abandoned the ether. Where Poincaré said convention, Einstein in 1905 said physics: adopt light-signal synchronization as the definition, wed it to the light postulate, and follow the logic wherever it goes. It goes somewhere genuinely strange: two observers in relative motion, each synchronizing clocks flawlessly by the same procedure, disagree about which distant events are simultaneous—and both are right.

Ask
Euler
Stuck? Scan this pageAsk Euler, your Fermi AI tutor, anything about this page — why a step works, the physical picture, or a worked example. It answers in your pocket.
Simultaneity3.2 · Synchronizing Clocks with Light

3.2Synchronizing Clocks with Light

Take the vaguest word in physics, “now,” and build a machine that pins it down. One inertial frame, two identical clocks at rest, clock A here and clock B a distance d_AB away, measured once with rigid rods. Each ticks at the same rate; what neither knows is the other’s offset. Synchronization means fixing that offset, and to fix it we must send a messenger—and Einstein chose the one messenger whose behavior we have elevated to a postulate: light. The protocol: at the instant A reads t_A, fire a flash toward B; it arrives when B reads t_B, reflects, and returns when A reads t′_A. The clocks are synchronized if the light spent equal times on the two legs: t_B − t_A = t′_A − t_B, i.e. t_B = (t_A + t′_A)/2.

There is an equivalent form we use constantly, the midpoint rule: two events are simultaneous if flashes emitted at each arrive together at the point midway between them. Flashes emitted at equal readings each cover d_AB/2 at speed c and arrive together at the midpoint—a single coincidence at a single place, which every observer can verify. The midpoint rule needs no clocks at the distant events, only a referee in the middle.

t_B = (t_A + t′_A)/23.2.1
t_B = t_A + ε(t′_A − t_A), 0 < ε < 13.2.2
c→ = c/2ε, c← = c/2(1−ε)3.2.3

Now the crucial point. The condition looks like an empirical claim—light takes equal times each way—but to time the outbound leg alone you need two synchronized clocks, and synchronizing them is the task at hand. The circle is airtight. The only speed that escapes it is the round-trip speed 2d_AB/(t′_A − t_A), clocked start to finish on the single clock A: no offset, no convention. Every experiment ever performed on the speed of light is, at bottom, a round-trip experiment. Reichenbach made the freedom explicit: one may set t_B = t_A + ε(t′_A − t_A) for any 0 < ε < 1, giving one-way speeds c→ = c/2ε and c← = c/2(1−ε). The two legs always sum to the same round-trip time, so every observation is untouched. What singles out ε = ½ is convenience wedded to the light postulate—the unique choice under which light picks out no preferred direction and synchrony is symmetric and transitive.

We adopt ε = ½ for the rest of the book, with eyes open: it is the sensible convention, not a discovered fact, and the honest phrasing is that observers in relative motion who each adopt this same convention will contradict each other. The modern test of the convention-free part is brutal: rotating optical resonators (Herrmann and collaborators, 2009) found the round-trip speed direction-independent to better than a part in 10¹⁷. Experiment nails the two-way speed to seventeen decimals, while the one-way speed remains a matter of convention no matter how good the apparatus.

Ask
Euler
Stuck? Scan this pageAsk Euler, your Fermi AI tutor, anything about this page — why a step works, the physical picture, or a worked example. It answers in your pocket.
Simultaneity3.3 · Lightning on the Train

3.3Lightning on the Train

Einstein staged his argument not in the austere 1905 paper but in the little 1917 book for lay readers, where a railway carriage and two bolts of lightning dismantle two centuries of Newtonian time in three pages. The stage: a long straight embankment is frame S, its lattice of synchronized clocks planted along the track; a train glides along it at constant v in the +x direction, frame S′. Two bolts strike the two ends of a carriage. Lightning is a superb tool because a strike is violent and local—each bolt scorches both the carriage end and the adjacent rail, converting each strike into an unambiguous coincidence, an event in the strict sense. Call the rear strike A and the front strike B. What is up for grabs is only the time coordinate each ledger assigns.

On the embankment stands M, midway between the two scorch marks by tape measure. On the train rides M′, at the midpoint of the carriage, equidistant by the train’s own rulers from the two scorched ends. One stipulation completes the setup and is the experiment’s only input datum: the two bolts are arranged so their flashes arrive at M at the same instant—a single coincidence readable off one clock. But while the flashes are in flight, the train carries M′ toward the point where B struck and away from where A struck. She was midpoint of the embankment marks for one instant only; of the marks on her own carriage she remains midpoint forever. The entire relativity of simultaneity lives in the gap between those two facts. Only two tools are permitted: the principle of relativity and the light postulate.

t_F = L_S/2(c+v), t_R = L_S/2(c−v)3.3.1
Δt = vL_S/(c² − v²)3.3.2
Δt′ = vL₀/c²3.3.3

M has the easy job. He stands midway between the marks; each flash reached him at c; equal distances at equal speed mean equal flight times; and the flashes arrived together. Working backward, he concludes the departures were simultaneous. In frame S, the two strikes happened at the same time—verdict one, and there is no flaw in it. Now M′. Described from the embankment, she rides toward the front flash and away from the rear, so she meets the front flash first—a coincidence-fact every frame honors. But how does she explain it? By the light postulate, applied in her frame with the same right M had, each flash traveled toward her at c—not c − v and c + v; that Newtonian escape hatch is welded shut. Equal distances, equal speed, and a staggered arrival, so she concludes the front strike happened first. In frame S′, the front bolt struck before the rear—verdict two, and there is no flaw in it either.

How big is the disagreement? Work in S, with strikes at t = 0, M at the origin, carriage ends at ±L_S/2, and M′ moving at +v toward the front strike. The front wavefront starts at +L_S/2 moving left; the rear at −L_S/2 chasing her. Setting each equal to her position x = vt gives t_F = L_S/2(c+v) and t_R = L_S/2(c−v), so the reception gap in S is Δt = vL_S/(c² − v²). Transferring to her frame (both receptions occur at M′, one clock) and inferring back through equal flight times, the front strike precedes the rear, in her frame, by Δt′ = vL₀/c² to first order. This little formula is the quantitative heart of the chapter, and it generalizes: two events simultaneous in S and separated by Δx along the motion are assigned by the moving frame a time offset vΔx/c², with the event ahead occurring earlier. For a 200 m TGV carriage at 89 m/s the offset is about 2×10⁻¹³ s—a fifth of a picosecond, the time light takes to cross a hair’s width—which is why two centuries of physicists found Newton’s absolute time flawless. The effect was never absent; it was merely 0.2 picoseconds tall.

Ask
Euler
Stuck? Scan this pageAsk Euler, your Fermi AI tutor, anything about this page — why a step works, the physical picture, or a worked example. It answers in your pocket.
Simultaneity3.4 · Reciprocity and the Transverse Case

3.4Reciprocity and the Transverse Case

Hand the pen to M′. By the first postulate she may regard her carriage as at rest and the platform as sliding backward at v. Let her audit two platform clocks A and B, separated by D, that the stationmaster synchronized by a midpoint flash. In her frame the platform moves in the −x′ direction: clock B drifts toward its wavefront and clock A flees, so t′_B = D/2(c+v) and t′_A = D/2(c−v), and t′_A − t′_B ≈ vD/c². Clock B is set to zero earlier in S′, so the train’s verdict is that the platform’s clocks are desynchronized by vD/c², with the clock at the rear of the platform’s motion running ahead.

Compare with the embankment’s verdict, that the train’s clocks are desynchronized by vL/c². State the rule physically and the asymmetry evaporates: each frame finds that the other frame’s leading clock lags, and its trailing clock leads, by v/c² per unit of separation. The same postulates, the same protocol, the same number. Neither analysis borrowed from the other; neither frame did anything the other could not. This is reciprocity, and it is total. Is the offset a pointer reading awaiting a good fiber link? No—each number is a comparison between two frames’ synchronization conventions, and a convention is not a needle on a dial. What experiment does see is the closed-loop face of the same first-order term: carry a timing signal around a loop on the rotating Earth and the eastward and westward transit times differ by a convention-free amount—the Sagnac effect, which global timekeeping budgets for daily. The relativity of simultaneity is real physics, but its measurable face is a loop, not a line.

t′_A − t′_B ≈ vD/c²3.4.1
τ = −τ ⇒ τ = 0 (transverse)3.4.2

Now the second stress test: separation across the motion. Plant two posts on opposite sides of the track, each a distance h from the centerline, and pop a firecracker on each; the stationmaster receives both bangs together and calls them simultaneous. What does the train say? No algebra is needed. The reflection y → −y maps the whole arrangement onto itself—the velocity points along x and is untouched—while swapping the two events. So if the train concluded the +y pop preceded the −y pop by τ, the reflection delivers an identical situation in which it follows by τ. One situation, two verdicts—unless τ = −τ, hence τ = 0. The pops are simultaneous in S′ as well: motion along the track cannot manufacture an asymmetry between two directions it does not distinguish. The disagreement is directional (first order in vΔx∥/c², transverse contributes nothing) and perfectly reciprocal—each frame convicts the other by the same formula.

Ask
Euler
Stuck? Scan this pageAsk Euler, your Fermi AI tutor, anything about this page — why a step works, the physical picture, or a worked example. It answers in your pocket.
Simultaneity3.5 · Drawing Spacetime: Events, World Lines, Light

3.5Drawing Spacetime: Events, World Lines, Light

The page has two axes: position x horizontal, and time up the page—multiplied by c, so the axis reads ct and carries the same units as the horizontal. This one cosmetic choice is the most consequential convention in the business. A flash of light, advancing one meter of x per meter of ct, becomes a line at exactly 45°: light, the referee of every synchronization argument, gets a fixed, frame-independent slope, and everything else arranges itself around it. The vocabulary is small. An event is a point; an object persisting through time is a curve, its world line. An object at rest has a vertical world line; one at constant velocity v has slope c/v. For anything material v < c, so every material world line is steeper than 45°; light’s slope of ±1 is the boundary of the diagram’s traffic law. A horizontal line would mean infinite speed, and the diagram makes the prohibition visible as geometry.

Plotting time as an axis was not Einstein’s innovation, nor Minkowski’s: nineteenth-century railway dispatchers ran their networks from graphical timetables and read a collision risk as a crossing that should not be there. But a timetable is a picture drawn in one frame. What Minkowski saw in 1908 is that the plane of x and ct has an intrinsic structure deeper than any frame, with light’s 45° lines playing a role every observer agrees on.

slope = c/v3.5.1
ct* = L/(1 − v/c)3.5.2

Worked example: a photon overtakes a passenger. In frame S a train moves at v = c/2; at t = 0 a flash is emitted from the rear of the carriage, at the origin, and a passenger sits a carriage length ahead, at x = L. The photon’s world line is x = ct; the passenger’s is x = L + vt, i.e. ct = 2(x − L). Setting them equal gives ct = 2L, x = 2L—read straight off the axes, or note the gap L closes at c − v = c/2, so the chase lasts ct = 2L. The general crossing is ct* = L/(1 − v/c): as v → 0 it drops to L; as v → c the two lines approach parallel and ct* → ∞. The teenage Einstein’s light-chasing question, answered by geometry: you cannot catch what your world line can never intersect. Three habits make every later figure legible—find the events first, order along a single world line by reading upward (every frame agrees on that lived chronology), and audit the slopes.

Ask
Euler
Stuck? Scan this pageAsk Euler, your Fermi AI tutor, anything about this page — why a step works, the physical picture, or a worked example. It answers in your pocket.
Simultaneity3.6 · The Train Experiment on the Page

3.6The Train Experiment on the Page

Sections of careful prose compress onto one spacetime diagram as five straight lines. Commit to numbers: in frame S the scorch marks are 600 m apart, rear strike A at x = 0, front strike B at x = 600 m, both at t = 0. M stands at x = 300 m; M′ is also at 300 m at t = 0, moving at v = 0.6c. That speed is absurd for a train—a real express has β = 10⁻⁷ and a world line indistinguishable from vertical—but exaggerating β makes the geometry visible. Draw, with equal scales: the embankment’s “now” (horizontal ct = 0, with A and B on it), M′s vertical world line, M′’s line of slope c/v = 5/3, and the two 45° flash rays from A and B.

The two flash rays and M′s vertical world line pass through the single point (ct, x) = (300, 300) m—a triple intersection. That one point is the platform verdict: both flashes arrive at M together, and the midpoint rule declares the strikes simultaneous. An entire paragraph condensed to three lines meeting at a point—and because an intersection is a coincidence, no frame will dispute that dot. Now follow M′’s tilted line. It crosses the front flash at ct = 187.5 m and the rear flash at ct = 750 m: she meets the front flash first, by a wide margin.

M′ world line: x = 300 + 0.6 ct (meters)3.6.1
c(t₂ − t₁) = βD/(1 − β²)3.6.2

Notice what the diagram has done to the argument. The relativity of simultaneity is no longer a subtle claim about observers and postulates; it is a fact of plane geometry: a tilted line launched from the midpoint of two equidistant 45° rays cannot cross them at the same height. Only the vertical line through the midpoint can. That is the whole theorem, provable with a ruler. And the two reception events sit at wildly different embankment positions—412.5 m and 750 m—yet both happen at M′, the same place in her frame. Her world line is her “same place,” just as a horizontal line is S’s “same time.” Which forces the final question: A and B lie on one horizontal line, S saying “simultaneous,” but in the carriage frame the front strike happened first, so wherever the carriage’s line of “now” lies on this page, it cannot be horizontal.

Ask
Euler
Stuck? Scan this pageAsk Euler, your Fermi AI tutor, anything about this page — why a step works, the physical picture, or a worked example. It answers in your pocket.
Simultaneity3.7 · The Tilt of “Now”

3.7The Tilt of “Now”

We will not guess where the carriage’s “now” lies; we will construct it, using only the synchronization protocol and the lines-and-intersections method. The easier axis first: the ct′ axis is the world line of a particle at rest at the carriage’s spatial origin—M′ herself—the line x = β ct, of slope 1/β. The x′ axis is where the real physics lives: it is the set of events with t′ = 0, every event the carriage judges simultaneous with the origin O. We possess one definition of distant simultaneity: the radar midpoint rule. Let M′ emit a photon at P = (−βa, −a) and receive it back at Q = (+βa, +a); by the midpoint rule the reflection event E is simultaneous, in her frame, with O. Intersecting the two 45° photon legs gives E = (a, βa). Vary a and E sweeps the full x′ axis: ct = βx.

This is the x′ axis: a straight line through the origin with slope v/c—not horizontal, but tilted up toward the light line by arctanβ, precisely the angle by which the ct′ axis tilted from the vertical. The two primed axes are mirror images in the 45° light line, and that symmetry is no accident: it is the light postulate drawn as geometry. A photon through O must lie midway between S′’s space and time axes, just as it lies midway between S’s. As v grows the primed axes scissor symmetrically toward the light line; as v → 0 they relax back into the perpendicular pair.

ct′ axis: x = β ct3.7.1
x′ axis: ct = βx3.7.2
ct = βx + const (S′ lines of now)3.7.3

Now the summit. A frame’s lines of simultaneity are the family parallel to its space axis: for S the horizontals, for S′ the family ct = βx + const. Lay this over the strikes. The carriage’s “now” through the front strike B passes below event A, so the front strike happened first in the carriage frame—the verdict now visible as one line failing to pass through one point. The size of the failure is slope times run: cΔt = βΔx, i.e. Δt = vΔx/c² = 1.2 μs for our numbers. The tilt of the simultaneity line is the vΔx/c² effect—not an analogy for it, but the same first-order quantity rendered as a slope. Simultaneity is not a property of the two events; it is a property of the slicing—of which family of parallel lines you use to layer spacetime into successive instants. The embankment slices horizontally and catches both strikes; the carriage slices at slope v/c and cannot. And be warned: these are scissors, not a turntable. A rigid rotation would carry the 45° light line to another slope, which the second postulate forbids; the axes scissor toward the light line, and the page’s Euclidean ruler does not calibrate them.

Ask
Euler
Stuck? Scan this pageAsk Euler, your Fermi AI tutor, anything about this page — why a step works, the physical picture, or a worked example. It answers in your pocket.
Simultaneity3.8 · No Universal “Now”: What Survives

3.8No Universal “Now”: What Survives

The tilt rule—the line of simultaneity for a frame at velocity v has slope β = v/c—lets v run over every velocity a material observer could have, −c < v < c. Through any single event P the candidate “nows” form a fan, ct = βx with −1 < β < 1, pivoting about P and pressing ever closer to the two 45° light lines without reaching them. Each line is some inertial frame’s honest answer to “which distant events happen at the same moment as P?” There are infinitely many answers, and the first postulate forbids crowning any. Notice where the fan lives: every member satisfies |ct| < |x|, so it tiles the region outside the light lines completely, and touches nothing inside.

Take Q at (ct, x) = (300, 500) m—spatially distant, barely later than P. A frame’s verdict on before-or-after is the sign of ct − βx. Poll three frames: β = 0 gives 300 > 0 (Q after P); β = 0.6 gives 0 (simultaneous); β = 0.8 gives −100 < 0 (Q before P). After, during, before—three flawless applications of the midpoint rule, three different temporal orders for the same pair. Now move the second event inside the light cone, Q* at (500, 300): for every |β| < 1, ct − βx = 500 − 300β > 200 > 0. No frame places Q* before P; to tilt a now-line up to Q* would require β = 5/3, no frame at all. Events inside the light cone keep their temporal order in every frame.

ct = βx, −1 < β < 1 (the fan of nows)3.8.1
|Δx| ≶ c|Δt| (spacelike / timelike)3.8.2

Why does the boundary fall at 45°? For P to cause Q would need an influence covering 500 m in 300 m of light-travel time—speed 5c/3. For P to reach Q* needs only 300 m in 500 m—speed 0.6c, an achievable errand. The events whose order the fan can scramble are precisely those no light-or-slower signal can connect. Timelike (|Δx| < c|Δt|): order absolute, a signal can link them. Spacelike (|Δx| > c|Δt|): order frame-dependent. That confinement is why causality survives the demolition. If an influence hopped to a spacelike event faster than light, some frame would see the effect precede its cause, and relaying such a signal through two frames could return a message to P before it was sent. Nature escapes with one clause: no signal travels faster than light—and then every pair whose order is negotiable is a pair that cannot converse.

Tally what the chapter destroyed and what it spared. Destroyed: the universal present, Newton’s time flowing “equably”; there is only the fan. Spared: every local coincidence; the light cone itself, since all frames draw light at 45°; every round-trip measurement; and the entire causal order of the world. What the postulates dismantled was never causation. It was only the assumption that “now” extends across space as a matter of fact rather than a matter of bookkeeping—a coordinate convention, like the prime meridian: indispensable for keeping records, arbitrary in its placement, and no ship has ever run aground on it. As Einstein wrote to Besso’s family weeks before his own death, “the distinction between past, present, and future is only a stubbornly persistent illusion.” What is real are the events and their causal relations; the slicing into past, now, and future belongs to the observer’s ledger, not to the world.

Ask
Euler
Stuck? Scan this pageAsk Euler, your Fermi AI tutor, anything about this page — why a step works, the physical picture, or a worked example. It answers in your pocket.
 Chapter IV
Chapter Four
IV

The Invariant Interval

The Invariant Interval4.1 · What Survives the Transformation?

4.1What Survives the Transformation?

Two surveyors map the same field. The first aligns her grid with true north; the second, whose compass is broken, aligns his with the line from the gate to the old oak, some 30° off. Each measures the separation between the same two fence posts. She reports Δx = 40 m east, Δy = 30 m north; he reports Δx′ = 49.64 m, Δy′ = 5.98 m. Compare their notebooks entry by entry and you would swear they surveyed different fields. But let each compute Δx² + Δy²: she gets 2500 m², he gets 2500.0 m², and both announce the posts are 50 m apart. The individual coordinates were artifacts of each grid; the distance belongs to the field. A rotation scrambles Δx and Δy into each other, yet one quadratic combination passes through unscathed—and conversely, the transformations preserving that combination are exactly the rotations. The invariant and the transformation group define each other.

A Lorentz boost along x is the relativistic surveyor problem, mixing time and space: Δt′ = γ(Δt − vΔx/c²), Δx′ = γ(Δx − vΔt). One observer’s Δt leaks into the other’s Δx and back. Simultaneity, duration, length—all scrambled. So we ask the question the parable trains us to ask: what combination survives? The Euclidean instinct, c²Δt² + Δx², fails—the cross terms reinforce. The combination that works carries a minus sign: Δs² = c²Δt² − Δx², the spacetime interval. The proof is direct substitution. Squaring the transformed separations and subtracting, the cross terms are identical (each −2γ²vΔtΔx) and cancel completely, leaving γ²(1 − v²/c²)(c²Δt² − Δx²). But γ² = 1/(1 − v²/c²), so the prefactor is 1: c²Δt′² − Δx′² = c²Δt² − Δx². The velocity v has vanished without a trace.

Δt′ = γ(Δt − vΔx/c²), Δx′ = γ(Δx − vΔt)4.1.1
Δs² = c²Δt² − Δx²4.1.2
Δs² = c²Δt² − Δx² − Δy² − Δz²4.1.3

Worked example: two events, two frames, one interval. In the lab a laser fires at the origin (event A) and a detector 600 m down the beamline clicks 5 μs later (event B), so Δx = 600 m and cΔt = 1500 m, giving Δs² = 1500² − 600² = 1,890,000 m². View from a rocket at v = 3c/5 (γ = 5/4): the boost gives cΔt′ = (5/4)(1500 − 0.6×600) = 1425 m and Δx′ = (5/4)(600 − 0.6×1500) = −375 m—Δx′ even flips sign, because the rocket outruns the gap. Yet Δs′² = 1425² − 375² = 1,890,000 m². The notebooks disagree on every entry and agree on the one that matters.

Extending to three dimensions costs almost nothing: a boost along x leaves transverse coordinates alone, so Δs² = c²Δt² − Δx² − Δy² − Δz² is the same value in every inertial frame—the one theorem this chapter needs. Note what it does not say: it does not say Δs² is positive. Unlike a Euclidean distance, the interval can be positive, negative, or zero, and the theorem protects the sign. That trichotomy is itself frame-independent, and it will organize the light cone and the timelike/spacelike/null classification, and through them the entire causal structure of the world.

Ask
Euler
Stuck? Scan this pageAsk Euler, your Fermi AI tutor, anything about this page — why a step works, the physical picture, or a worked example. It answers in your pocket.
The Invariant Interval4.2 · The Minus Sign: Anatomy of the Cancellation

4.2The Minus Sign: Anatomy of the Cancellation

A proof tells you that something is true; an anatomy lesson tells you why. In the variables β = v/c and ct, the boost is perfectly symmetric: ct′ = γ(ct − βx), x′ = γ(x − β ct). The same coefficient −β governs both directions of mixing—space leaks into time as strongly as time into space. Square each, and the cross term −2γ²β ct x appears in both with the same sign. Cross terms with the same sign can only be destroyed by subtracting: (ct′)² − (x′)² = γ²(1 − β²)(c²t² − x²) = c²t² − x². The minus sign did two jobs at once: it killed the cross terms and it arranged the leftover diagonal terms to share the factor (1 − β²), ready to be cancelled by γ² in one stroke. The minus sign is not decoration; it is the entire machine.

Try instead the Euclidean sum: (ct′)² + (x′)² = γ²[(1 + β²)(c²t² + x²) − 4β ct x] ≠ c²t² + x² unless β = 0. In the sum the identical cross terms reinforce, and the diagonal terms pick up a factor (1 + β²) that γ² cannot absorb. Why does ordinary rotation preserve the sum? Because rotation mixes antisymmetrically—+sinθ in one slot, −sinθ in the other—so its cross terms cancel in the sum. Rotations mix antisymmetrically; boosts mix symmetrically. The minus sign in Δs² is the algebraic shadow of the light cone.

ct′ = γ(ct − βx), x′ = γ(x − β ct)4.2.1
(ct′)² − (x′)² = c²t² − x²4.2.2
signature (+,−,−,−)4.2.3
Scan →
derivation
▶ Watch it derivedScan to play the The invariant interval, and its minus sign as a short video — every line above built on screen, step by step, in the book’s own hand.

There is a deeper way to say this, from nineteenth-century algebra. Every real quadratic form can be diagonalized to pure squares with coefficients +1, −1, or 0; the interval’s sign pattern (+,−,−,−) is its signature. Could some ingenious change of coordinates convert it to the Euclidean (+,+,+,+)? Sylvester’s law of inertia (1852) says a hard no: however you diagonalize by any invertible real change of variables, the counts of + and − signs and zeros are the same. The signature is intrinsic, immune to every rotation, boost, and transformation anyone might invent. Spacetime is not Euclidean four-space in disguise; the single minus sign is the whole difference. (The old trick x₄ = ict makes the interval look Euclidean, but Sylvester governs real changes of variables, and the disguise hides exactly the null cone and causal structure that matter—“Farewell to ict.”)

If this seems an algebraic curiosity, walk into the counting room at CERN. Every proton–proton collision at the LHC is born with a different, unknown boost along the beamline—each event hands the experimenters data from a different frame, and nobody is told which. Plot any Euclidean-style combination of a Higgs decay’s photon energies and angles and millions of events smear into a featureless continuum. But compute the minus-sign combination E² − |p|²c² for the photon pair and every Higgs decay returns the identical answer: the parent’s mass, near 125 GeV/c², piling up as a sharp peak. That peak, announced on 4 July 2012, exists because Sylvester’s signature guarantees that one quadratic combination ignores the private boost of every event.

Ask
Euler
Stuck? Scan this pageAsk Euler, your Fermi AI tutor, anything about this page — why a step works, the physical picture, or a worked example. It answers in your pocket.
The Invariant Interval4.3 · How Much the Light Cone Alone Forces

4.3How Much the Light Cone Alone Forces

The substitution proof was honest but backwards. Historically and logically the transformation is the derived object and the light cone is the datum—the one thing experiment insists on directly. So play a stripped-down game. An observer knows a single fact: a flash of light expands as a sphere of radius ct in every inertial frame. She has never seen a γ. How much of Δs′² = Δs² can she reconstruct? All of it—but not from the light cone alone. Four ingredients: constancy of c (Δs² = 0 implies Δs′² = 0), homogeneity (no place or instant privileged), isotropy (no direction privileged), and reciprocity (if S′ moves at v in S, then S moves at −v in S′). Each is spent at an identifiable moment.

Homogeneity buys linearity: a map whose local action is everywhere the same is affine, and with origins aligned, linear. The light cone buys proportionality: writing the primed interval as the most general quadratic form and demanding it vanish on every null displacement collapses thirteen constants to one, Δs′² = λ(v)Δs²—but a universe with λ = 2 would satisfy everything so far. Isotropy buys λ(−v) = λ(v): rotations preserve both ct and |r|², so λ depends only on speed. Reciprocity buys λ² = 1: transform out and straight back, the round trip must be the identity, so λ = ±1, and continuity from the identity at v = 0 picks +1. Hence Δs′² = Δs²—recovered without ever writing a boost, a γ, or a velocity-addition formula.

Δs′² = λ(v) Δs²4.3.1
λ(v) = ±1 ⇒ λ ≡ 14.3.2

This bookkeeping retraces Einstein’s own steps. In §3 of the 1905 paper the transformation emerges carrying exactly this undetermined factor—he calls it φ(v)—and he eliminates it with the same isotropy and reciprocity arguments, in the same order. The honest slogan, then, is not “the interval is invariant because the speed of light is constant”: constancy of c fixes only the light cone, and a zero set determines a quadratic form only up to a factor, and only if the map is linear. Homogeneity, isotropy, and reciprocity do the rest. (A striking postscript: Zeeman proved in 1964 that any bijection of spacetime preserving causal order—no linearity or continuity assumed—must be a Lorentz transformation with a translation and a dilation. The cone is very nearly the whole story.)

Ask
Euler
Stuck? Scan this pageAsk Euler, your Fermi AI tutor, anything about this page — why a step works, the physical picture, or a worked example. It answers in your pocket.
The Invariant Interval4.4 · Invariant Hyperbolae and the Speed Limit

4.4Invariant Hyperbolae and the Speed Limit

What does the invariance theorem look like on a picture? Fix an origin event O and plot events on axes (x, ct). All events sharing a given interval from O lie on the curve c²t² − x² = Δs²—a hyperbola when Δs² ≠ 0, a pair of straight lines ct = ±x when Δs² = 0. Since a boost cannot change Δs², a boost can only slide an event along its own hyperbola, one point per observer, the whole curve traced out as β sweeps. A supernova at (ct, x) = (10, 8) ly has interval 100 − 64 = 36 ly²; boost to β = +0.5 and it lands at (6.93, 3.46); at β = +0.9, at (6.42, −2.29), behind the observer; at β = −0.5, at (16.17, 15.01). No two observers agree about anything separately, and every pair of coordinates satisfies c²t′² − x′² = 36.

Look at where that hyperbola is headed. For large ct and x it hugs the lines ct = ±x ever more closely, because c²t² − x² = (ct − x)(ct + x) = 36 forces ct − x → 0 as ct + x → ∞. Those light lines are the asymptotes, and a hyperbola never reaches them. Here, stated once and pointed to forever after, is the geometric form of the speed limit. Boosting harder slides an event closer to the light lines; but the light lines belong to a different invariant class, c²t² − x² = 0, and no boost, nor any stack of boosts, moves a point from one invariant set onto another. The asymptote is not a place on the hyperbola.

c²t² − x² = Δs²4.4.1
(ct − x)(ct + x) = Δs²4.4.2

One promissory note. Rotations slide points around circles x² + y² = const by trading x for y through sines and cosines; boosts slide events along hyperbolae by trading ct for x through a pair of functions obeying a difference-of-squares identity. Those functions are cosh and sinh, the trade is a hyperbolic rotation, and the additive angle it carries—the rapidity—is the natural currency of boosts.

Ask
Euler
Stuck? Scan this pageAsk Euler, your Fermi AI tutor, anything about this page — why a step works, the physical picture, or a worked example. It answers in your pocket.
The Invariant Interval4.5 · The Light Cone

4.5The Light Cone

The asymptotes deserve their own section, because assembled across all of spacetime they form the single most important structure in relativity. Fix an event E and ask which events are separated from it by a null interval, Δs² = 0. With one spatial dimension the condition is Δx = ±cΔt: two 45° lines. Add a second spatial dimension and a fixed-time slice becomes a circle of radius cΔt—the expanding light flash. Stack the slices and the ripples trace a cone opening upward at 45°, apex at E; the past-time slices build a mirror cone opening downward, the set of events from which a flash arrives exactly at E. In full spacetime the slices are spheres: the future half is the expanding spherical wavefront of light emitted at E, the past half the collapsing shell of all light converging on E.

The cone carves all of spacetime, relative to E, into three regions: the interior of the future cone (Δs² > 0, Δt > 0), the interior of the past cone (Δs² > 0, Δt < 0), and the exterior, everything with Δs² < 0, for which Eddington coined the perfect name: the absolute elsewhere. Every inertial observer draws the same three regions. Whether an event lies inside, on, or outside is a question about the sign of Δs², which is invariant. And the future/past split is protected too: for an event inside the future cone, |Δx| < cΔt, so under a boost cΔt′ = γ(cΔt − βΔx) stays positive. Inside the cone, before and after are absolute.

Δx = ±cΔt (null cone)4.5.1
cΔt′ = γ(cΔt − βΔx)4.5.2

Nothing about E was special. Every event carries its own double cone, all congruent, all oriented the same way, all agreed upon by every inertial observer—a cone field, the invariant scaffolding of special relativity. Euclidean space has nothing like it: around a point every direction is equivalent, but Minkowski spacetime hands every event an absolute local structure—these directions lead to the future, those to the past, the rest to the elsewhere. When we reach general relativity, the whole theory of gravitation can be summarized as the story of what happens when these cones tilt and flex from point to point. On 21 September 1908 Minkowski opened his Cologne lecture: “Henceforth space by itself, and time by itself, are doomed to fade away into mere shadows, and only a kind of union of the two will preserve an independent reality.”

Ask
Euler
Stuck? Scan this pageAsk Euler, your Fermi AI tutor, anything about this page — why a step works, the physical picture, or a worked example. It answers in your pocket.
The Invariant Interval4.6 · Timelike, Spacelike, Null

4.6Timelike, Spacelike, Null

The cone carved spacetime into regions; the regions need names attached to numbers. Given two events, form the invariant interval Δs² = c²Δt² − Δx². Three cases exhaust the possibilities: > 0 timelike (inside the cone), = 0 null (on the cone), < 0 spacelike (elsewhere). Because Δs² is invariant, this classification is not a matter of frame—no boost, however violent, can drag an event pair from one class to another. Timelike, spacelike, null: the first genuinely frame-independent adjectives relativity has handed us. What does each feel like, in clocks and metersticks?

Timelike (the wristwatch). If Δs² > 0 then |Δx/Δt| < c: something slower than light could travel from one event to the other. Boost to the frame of such a traveler, v = Δx/Δt, and Δx′ = 0—both events happen at the same place, and a single clock attends both. The interval reads Δs² = c²Δt′², so Δτ = Δs/c, the proper time: the time between the events as told by the one clock present at both. Every observer measures different Δt and Δx, but all recover the same Δτ from the combination. For a clock moving at v = βc this gives Δτ = Δt/γ—time dilation is the statement that proper time is invariant while coordinate time is not.

Δs² > 0 timelike, = 0 null, < 0 spacelike4.6.1
Δτ = Δs/c = Δt/γ4.6.2
Δσ = √(−Δs²) = √(Δx² − c²Δt²)4.6.3

Spacelike (the meterstick). If Δs² < 0, then |Δx| > c|Δt|: too far apart in space for their separation in time. No clock can attend both—the boost that would bring them to the same place exceeds c. Instead boost with v = c²Δt/Δx, legal precisely because |cΔt| < |Δx|, and the time separation vanishes: there is a frame in which the events are simultaneous, and in it the interval is a pure distance, Δσ = √(−Δs²) = √(Δx² − c²Δt²), the proper distance a surveyor measures with a meterstick. And notice: as v sweeps past that value, the sign of Δt′ flips. For spacelike pairs even the order of the events is frame-dependent. That sounds like a paradox begging to demolish causality; the causality section shows why it is instead causality’s salvation.

Null (light’s silent clock). Finally Δs² = 0: the events sit on each other’s light cones, connectable by a light ray and nothing slower. Both instruments fail—proper time and proper distance are each zero. A photon leaves a quasar ten billion light-years away; ten billion years later it ends its journey in a retina, and the interval between emission and absorption is c²Δt² − c²Δt² = 0: not small, zero, and zero in every frame. There is no frame moving with light, so “the photon’s point of view” is not a frame we may occupy; but take any timelike traveler between fixed departure and arrival and let β → 1, and the proper time shrinks toward zero without limit. In the geometry of spacetime, the quasar event and the retina event have no separation at all—which is why the null cone, not any surface of constant distance, is the true boundary of locality.

Ask
Euler
Stuck? Scan this pageAsk Euler, your Fermi AI tutor, anything about this page — why a step works, the physical picture, or a worked example. It answers in your pocket.
The Invariant Interval4.7 · Spacetime Diagrams as Working Tools

4.7Spacetime Diagrams as Working Tools

Everything established so far can be drawn quantitatively, so that simultaneity, time dilation, and length contraction can be read off a figure with a straightedge and no algebra. The spacetime diagram is to relativity what the free-body diagram is to mechanics: not an illustration but an instrument. Time runs vertically as ct, so light travels along 45° lines and a massive particle at speed v traces a worldline of slope c/v > 1—slower means steeper, a particle at rest is vertical, and the unattainability of c becomes a rule of draftsmanship: no worldline may tilt down to the diagonal. A second observer moving at βc has her ct′ axis along x = β ct (tilted from vertical by tanθ = β) and her x′ axis along ct = βx (tilted from horizontal by the same angle). The two primed axes are mirror images across the 45° light line, scissoring together onto it as β → 1. They are not perpendicular on the page—but that is not a defect; they are exactly as orthogonal in the Minkowski sense as the unprimed pair.

Tilting the axes is easy; the subtle step is calibrating the ticks, and here the hyperbolae earn their keep. Draw the unit hyperbolae c²t² − x² = ±1; because the interval is invariant, the same curves are c²t′² − x′² = ±1, belonging to no observer and to all. Where the upward hyperbola crosses the ct′ axis is the event ct′ = 1; where the rightward one crosses the x′ axis is x′ = 1. Explicitly the tick ct′ = 1 sits at lab coordinates (ct, x) = (γ, γβ)—the boosted image of (1, 0), the moving clock’s first tick, exactly where it belongs. One family of curves calibrates every set of axes at once.

ct′ axis: x = β ct; x′ axis: ct = βx4.7.1
tick at (ct, x) = (γ, γβ)4.7.2
page-distance = √((1 + β²)/(1 − β²))4.7.3

Three cornerstone results become acts of projection. Relativity of simultaneity: the moving observer’s now-slices tilt upward with slope β, so two lab-simultaneous events are met at different heights—read straight off the tilt. Time dilation, both ways: follow the moving clock to its tick at (γβ, γ) and the lab assigns it ct = γ > 1; reverse the projection and the lab tick at (0, 1) lands at ct′ = γ. Each finds the other’s clock slow, and the paradox dissolves because the two comparisons use visibly different slices of the page. Length contraction: a rod at rest sweeps a vertical band, and the moving observer intersects it with one of her now-slices to read x′ = L/γ—shorter, because “the rod, now” means a different slice.

One warning, the single most common error. Do not read the tilted axes with a ruler. The tick ct′ = 1 sits at Euclidean page-distance √((1 + β²)/(1 − β²)) from the origin—about 1.46 for β = 0.6, growing without bound as the axes close on the light line. Calibrate by transferring tick spacing with a ruler and every reading is wrong; you will “derive” time contraction and length dilation and wonder where the theory failed. Nothing failed but the ruler. The page is a Euclidean surface conscripted to display a non-Euclidean geometry; only the interval means something, and the hyperbola, not the ruler, calibrates the axes—at every β, on every diagram you will ever draw.

Ask
Euler
Stuck? Scan this pageAsk Euler, your Fermi AI tutor, anything about this page — why a step works, the physical picture, or a worked example. It answers in your pocket.
The Invariant Interval4.8 · Causality as Geometry

4.8Causality as Geometry

Every law of physics presupposes an ordering—the force causes the acceleration, the decay precedes the detection. If observers could disagree about which of two causally connected events came first, physics would be incoherent. Simultaneity is a frame-dependent bookkeeping convention; that result should have worried you. This section is the summit, and the answer is the light cone. The question “can event A influence event B?”—about signals and forces—turns out to be exactly “does B lie inside or on the future cone of A?”—about geometry. Since the cone is built from the invariant Δs², the geometric question has the same answer in every frame. Causality is safe precisely because it is geometry.

Take A and B with Δt > 0. The sign of Δt′ is the sign of cΔt − βΔx, which flips only if some attainable β drives it through zero, at β* = cΔt/Δx. Everything hinges on whether β* is smaller or larger than 1—on the trichotomy. Timelike pairs never flip: if |Δx| < cΔt the flip would need |β*| > 1, a boost past light. Null pairs never flip either. Spacelike pairs always flip: β* lies strictly between 0 and 1, a legal boost, and observers beyond it find B before A. The classification was not idle taxonomy; it was causal triage.

β* = cΔt/Δx (order flips beyond this, spacelike only)4.8.1
cΔt′ = γΔt(c − βu) < 0 when β > c/u4.8.2

At first the spacelike flip looks like the catastrophe we set out to avoid. But the spacelike condition says a signal from A to B would need speed Δx/Δt > c. No signal, no influence connected these events—they are strangers, and asking “which stranger acted first?” has no physical content, so nature is free to let the answer vary. Relativity does not protect the order of every pair; it protects the order of every pair that could be cause and effect, and that is exactly the set inside or on the light cone. The disagreement lives only where it cannot do harm. Run the logic in reverse and the cone is the speed limit’s enforcement: a signal at u > c makes emission and reception spacelike, and every observer moving faster than c/u watches it arrive before it was sent—not an illusion but that frame’s honest coordinates. Let Alice send such a message and Bob reply instantaneously in his frame, and the reply reaches Alice before she sent the question: the tachyonic antitelephone. Faster-than-light plus relativity does not equal exotic engineering; it equals self-contradiction. The speed limit is not an add-on to relativity. It is the causality theorem, drawn as a cone.

Ask
Euler
Stuck? Scan this pageAsk Euler, your Fermi AI tutor, anything about this page — why a step works, the physical picture, or a worked example. It answers in your pocket.
The Invariant Interval4.9 · Light Cones in Cosmology

4.9Light Cones in Cosmology

Everything so far has been proved on paper. It is fair to ask whether the null cone is a physicist’s abstraction or a structure the universe respects. On 17 August 2017 the universe answered, across 130 million light-years. The LIGO and Virgo detectors recorded a hundred-second crescendo of gravitational waves from two neutron stars merging in NGC 4993; then, 1.74 seconds later, the Fermi satellite registered a short gamma-ray burst from the same patch of sky. The travel time was about 4.1×10¹⁵ s, so attributing the whole 1.7 s gap to a speed difference gives a bound |ε| ≲ 4×10⁻¹⁶. Gravity and light ride the same null cone to better than a part in 10¹⁵; one afternoon of data ruled out entire families of modified-gravity theories.

In this chapter’s language, the merger is an event E, its future light cone is the locus Δs² = 0, and the detection on Earth—130 million years up the page of a cosmic spacetime diagram—sits on that cone, confirmed by two independent messengers. The cone is not drawn on the universe by us. It is drawn by the universe, and it is the same cone for everything massless. The deeper cosmological lesson: our past light cone is the totality of observable astronomy. Every photon arriving tonight completes a null journey—zero interval, however many billions of years of coordinate time it spanned—so to receive light from an object is to receive it from an event on our past cone, and the farther the object, the further into the past that event sits.

|ε| ≲ 4×10⁻¹⁶ (GW170817 speed-difference bound)4.9.1
T = D/c ≈ 4.1×10¹⁵ s4.9.2

There is no way to see Andromeda as it is now; there is only Andromeda 2.5 million years ago, when the photons now entering your pupil left it. A quasar shows the universe’s adolescence; the cosmic microwave background is light that last scattered when the universe was a 3000-kelvin plasma only 380,000 years old—very nearly the bottom of our past cone, the oldest light there is. “Looking out is looking back” is sometimes presented as an inconvenience of finite light speed. It is the opposite: it is the reason cosmology is an empirical science at all. Because the past cone slices through every epoch, astronomers can watch the history of the universe rather than infer it. The light cone is the archive. One honest caution: every cone here is drawn in flat spacetime, and the real universe expands, so on cosmological scales the cones tilt and flare from event to event—stitching the local cones into a global causal structure is precisely the business of general relativity.
